routes.title: Banyoles Ardennes Long
routes.description: This is a demanding road cycling loop based out of Banyoles — the lakeside town in the Girona province of Catalunya, famous for hosting the 1992 Barcelona Olympics rowing events. The route heads north and east into the rolling foothills of the Garrotxa and Pla de l'Estany regions before looping back through a series of undulating climbs. Despite a relatively modest maximum elevation of 211m, the route earns its difficulty through the accumulation of repeated climbs across nearly 100km, giving it an 'ardennes' character with punchy, rolling terrain rather than one or two epic cols.
routes.detailedDescription:
Km 0–8 — Girona warm-up. The route departs from the eastern side of Banyoles lake at around 69m and begins a gentle roll northward through quiet back roads. This section is relatively flat with minor undulation.

Km 8.6–18.8 — First sustained climb (~91m gain over 10km). A steady rise through wooded terrain northeast of Banyoles. Average gradient ~0.9% with steeper pockets.

Km 20–26 — Mieres climb (~63m gain). A short, punchy 2km climb averaging ~2.9% — classic Ardennes-style terrain.

Km 26–41 — Rolling descent and plains. The route drops toward the Fluvià valley (24–33m elevation) with long straight fast sections.

Km 41–53 — Undulating recovery. A mid-length climb (~44m gain) around km 41–44 before skirting the southern edge of Alta Garrotxa.

Km 53.7–59.6 — Biggest climb of the day (~123m over ~6km). A sustained 2% average gradient into the Garrotxa hills with forested ridgelines.

Km 60–64 — Rocacorba area (211m high point). The route reaches its maximum elevation on the lower flanks of Rocacorba — not the full summit climb.

Km 64–80 — Rolling Rocacorba/Collsacabra foothills. Elevated terrain (140–190m) with continuous rolling rather than a simple descent.

Km 80.4–81.5 — Final punchy climb (~31m gain). A late short wall typical of Ardennes-style profiles.

Km 82–95.4 — Long descent to Banyoles. A 13km return to the lake at ~69m, finishing near popular cyclist cafés.

routes.title: Banyoles Hills
routes.description: This route is a compact inland Girona climbing loop focused on the rolling hill systems around Banyoles. Compared to the longer “Banyoles Ardennes Long” route, this version is shorter and more controlled, but still delivers a surprisingly high climbing load through continuous terrain changes.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast & Flowing. The opening section features smooth agricultural roads, fast rolling terrain, and gentle elevation changes. This terrain encourages high speed and allows groups to establish rhythm quickly. However, climbs begin appearing sooner than expected. 2. Transition Into Banyoles Hills. As the route approaches Banyoles, roads narrow, terrain becomes increasingly irregular, and climbing frequency rises sharply. This section defines the ride with almost constant elevation change despite the absence of huge climbs. 3. Key Climbs & Terrain Features. The core terrain consists of repeated medium-length climbs, frequent short ramps, and technical rhythm changes. Typical climbs range from 1–4 km at gradients of 4–7%, with occasional steeper pitches exiting villages. These climbs are ideal for tempo work but become fatiguing due to their repetition. 4. Rocacorba Foothill Terrain. The route profile strongly suggests movement through the lower Rocacorba region or nearby inland hill systems. This terrain includes long drags, false flats, and sustained seated climbing. Even without summiting Rocacorba itself, the surrounding roads accumulate elevation rapidly. 5. Ride Character. This is not a route defined by one major climb. Instead, fatigue builds through repetition, with climbs arriving continuously and recovery windows remaining short. The difficulty comes from sustained aerobic pressure, repeated threshold efforts, and constant terrain management. 6. Descents. Descents are short, flowing, and often technical through wooded sections. Unlike major mountain descents, they rarely provide full recovery, with another climb usually beginning shortly afterward.

routes.title: Coastal Route Alternative
routes.description: This route is a classic Costa Brava coastal endurance ride, blending inland Girona roads with the rugged Mediterranean coastline. Compared to the other routes you shared, this one is less of a pure mountain stage and more of a high-tempo rolling coastal route with relentless short climbing.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast Rolling Roads. The route begins with smooth inland roads leaving Girona, featuring gradual terrain that allows groups to settle and frequent false flats rather than major climbs. 2. Coastal Transition – Signature Section. The defining feature of this route is the move onto the Costa Brava coastal roads. Once near the coast, the terrain becomes continuously undulating, climbs become shorter but steeper, and recovery between efforts is minimal. This section is extremely scenic, physically deceptive, and harder than elevation totals suggest. 3. Key Climbs on the Route. This route strongly aligns with the classic Sant Grau coastal approach, typically featuring a 7–8 km climb averaging 5–6% with multiple rhythm changes. Sant Grau is famous because it links inland Girona with the coast, offers panoramic sea views higher up, and is heavily used by pro riders training around Girona. 4. Coastal Rollers (Tossa / Sant Feliu Region). After the main climb, the road constantly rises and falls. Short climbs often hit 7–10%, especially exiting coves and coastal bends. These repeated efforts accumulate fatigue rapidly. 5. Inland Return Section. Returning inland, roads become smoother and faster, with more gradual climbs and opportunities for sustained tempo riding. However, fatigue from the coast makes even mild gradients feel harder. 6. Scenery & Road Feel. Highlights include Mediterranean sea views, cliffside roads, pine forest descents, and quiet inland Catalunya villages.

routes.title: Girona Coastal: St Grau & ElsÀngels
routes.description: This route is a classic Girona benchmark ride, combining the rugged Costa Brava coastline with two of the region’s most iconic climbs: Sant Grau d’Ardenya and Els Àngels. It is one of the most balanced Girona routes, featuring coastal terrain, sustained climbing, fast inland tempo roads, and a long endurance feel without extreme elevation.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit – Fast Rolling Terrain. The route begins with smooth inland roads leaving Girona, gentle rollers, and an opportunity to settle into rhythm. This opening section typically feels fast and controlled, but it can encourage overpacing when riding in a group. Gradually, the terrain becomes hillier approaching the coast. 2. Coastal Transition – Costa Brava Terrain. Once near the coast, roads narrow, elevation changes become more frequent, and the route becomes rhythmically demanding. The coastal section includes repeated short climbs, technical descents, and cliffside roads with Mediterranean sea views. This terrain creates constant power fluctuations and produces fatigue faster than expected. 3. Sant Grau d’Ardenya. One of the defining climbs of Girona cycling, Sant Grau typically measures around 7–8 km with an average gradient of 5–6%. The climb is variable and rolling rather than brutally steep. Its constantly changing gradients repeatedly interrupt rhythm, while upper sections become more exposed. Sant Grau is famous for its Mediterranean views, technical approach roads, and its role linking Girona’s inland terrain with the Costa Brava coastline. It remains a staple climb for professional riders training around Girona. 4. Els Àngels. The second signature climb of the route, Els Àngels is approximately 10 km long with a steady 3–4% average gradient. Unlike Sant Grau, Els Àngels is smooth and controlled, making it ideal for sustained seated climbing and consistent pacing. The climb is iconic due to its popularity among Girona-based professionals, sheltered tree-lined sections, and suitability for tempo and threshold efforts. By the time riders reach Els Àngels, fatigue from the coastal section is already significant, making the climb feel more demanding than the numbers suggest. 5. Descents. The route includes two distinct descent styles. Coastal descents are technical, twisty, and require attention and bike handling skills, while inland descents are faster, more flowing, and better suited for recovery and speed maintenance.

routes.title: Les Planes d’Hostoles → Osor → Sant Hilari Sacalm
routes.description: This route is a deep inland Catalunya mountain ride centered around one of the most iconic cycling roads in the Girona region: the Osor Road and the Sant Hilari Sacalm climb system. Compared to the coastal routes, this is a far more mountainous, sustained, and rhythm-based climbing experience, featuring long uninterrupted climbs through dense forest roads with very little traffic.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Exit & Volcanic Zone Approach. The opening section heads northwest toward the Garrotxa volcanic region and the Les Planes d’Hostoles area. Terrain initially consists of rolling agricultural roads, gradual elevation gain, and fast pacing opportunities. The riding here feels smooth, efficient, and highly aerobic, although the climbing load progressively builds throughout the route. 2. Osor Road – The Signature Feature. The Osor Road is one of the most famous cycling roads in the Girona region and forms the defining feature of this ride. It is characterized by long sustained ascents, smooth gradients, and continuous forest-covered climbing. The road is highly regarded because traffic is extremely low, road surface quality is excellent, and the climbing rhythm is exceptionally consistent. Unlike coastal climbs, there are very few interruptions, allowing riders to settle into sustained tempo efforts. Typical climbing profiles involve 15–25 km of gradual uphill terrain, with most gradients ranging from 3–6% and rarely becoming excessively steep. This creates a pure endurance climbing experience focused more on aerobic stress than explosive fatigue. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Region. The route likely reaches or passes through Sant Hilari Sacalm, one of the highest inland plateaus near Girona. The region is famous for cool forest roads and is a popular destination for summer climbing rides. Terrain characteristics include long drags, false flats, and sustained seated climbing, rewarding riders who can maintain steady pacing over extended periods. 4. Descents. Descents on this route are long, fast, and flowing. Compared to coastal descents, they are generally less technical, feature more predictable corners, and make it easier to maintain speed. This provides better recovery opportunities and supports high-speed endurance riding over long distances.

routes.title: Olot → El Far → Coll d’Úria
routes.description: This route is a high-quality volcanic-region climbing loop through the Garrotxa area of Catalunya, linking together two of the region’s most respected inland climbs: El Far and Coll d’Úria. Compared to the Girona coastal rides, this route is more mountainous, more sustained, and more climber-oriented. It has a distinctly quiet inland Catalunya feel, with a high-aerobic climbing character and a hidden pro-training-road atmosphere.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Olot & Volcanic Region Opening. The ride begins around Olot, an area famous for its volcanic landscapes, quiet mountain roads, and dense forest terrain. The opening terrain includes rolling roads through the Garrotxa region, gradual elevation gain, and fast valley riding before the major climbs begin. This first phase feels smooth, scenic, and controlled, but the route escalates quickly as the climbing intensifies. 2. El Far. El Far is one of the signature climbs of inland Catalunya and forms a major highlight of the route. The climb features a long sustained ascent with scenic cliffside upper sections and gradients generally ranging between 4–7%, making it steadier than many coastal climbs. What makes El Far special is its combination of forest climbing, open exposed viewpoints, and highly rhythmic pacing. It is particularly well suited to tempo climbing, threshold intervals, and long seated efforts. The upper sections near Santuari del Far provide spectacular panoramic views, dramatic rock formations, and one of the finest viewpoints in the Girona region. 3. Coll d’Úria. Coll d’Úria is the more rugged and isolated climbing section of the route. It features a longer remote mountain ascent with more variable gradients and quiet forest roads. Compared to El Far, it is less famous, more isolated, and has a purer mountain-road climbing feel. The climb rewards controlled pacing, cadence discipline, and strong aerobic efficiency over extended periods. 4. Descents. Descents throughout the route are long, fast, and flowing. Highlights include technical upper mountain sections and high-speed lower valley exits. Unlike the Costa Brava coastal roads, these inland mountain roads are generally wider, with more predictable cornering rhythm and easier momentum preservation. 5. Overall Ride Character. This is one of the most scenic inland mountain routes and one of the purest climber-oriented rides in the collection. It feels remote, rhythmic, and purpose-built for endurance climbers. The route rewards patience, fueling discipline, and sustainable power output over long periods. Olot → El Far → Coll d’Úria delivers a true Catalunya volcanic mountain epic experience, combining deep forest climbing, long sustained ascents, quiet mountain roads, and a pure endurance climbing rhythm.

routes.title: Olot Loop
routes.description: This route is a classic volcanic-region endurance loop centered around the inland mountain roads surrounding Olot in the heart of the Garrotxa region. Compared to larger mountain-stage routes, this ride is more compact, more rhythm-based, and less extreme individually, while still becoming highly demanding over time. It is a quintessential inland Catalunya climber’s route, designed for aerobic endurance and quiet mountain-road training.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Olot & Garrotxa Opening. The route begins around Olot, a region famous for volcanic landscapes, dense forests, quiet mountain roads, and smooth flowing climbing terrain. The opening kilometers feature rolling valley roads, gradual elevation gain, and fast warm-up terrain. Initially, the riding feels calm, scenic, and efficient before gradually transitioning into more mountainous terrain with longer sustained climbs. 2. Garrotxa Mountain Roads. The defining terrain of this ride consists of continuous medium-length climbing, forest-covered ascents, and rhythmic gradients. Typical climbs range from 3–8 km with gradients around 4–7%, including occasional steeper ramps on transitions. This terrain is ideal for tempo climbing, aerobic endurance, and sustainable seated efforts over long durations. 3. Volcanic Region Elevation Changes. Unlike the sharper rhythm changes of the Girona coastal rides, the terrain here is smoother, more consistent, and progressively flowing. Climbs feel natural and uninterrupted, creating long periods under aerobic load with fewer explosive changes in rhythm. This is classic climber-friendly “diesel engine” terrain, rewarding riders who can sustain steady power output. 4. Descents. Descents are long, flowing, and generally less technical than those found along the coast. Key characteristics include predictable corners, good visibility, and extended high-speed sections. These descents allow efficient recovery and support a consistently high rolling speed throughout the route. 5. Overall Impression. This route is highly refined, exceptionally rideable, and quietly difficult. While it lacks famous summit finishes or brutally steep gradients, it delivers continuous climbing quality, exceptional road rhythm, and deep endurance fatigue over time. The ride ultimately feels much bigger than the raw numbers suggest and is deeply satisfying for experienced climbers seeking sustained aerobic mountain riding.

routes.title: Rocacorba (Girona → Banyoles → Rocacorba)
routes.description: This route is a true Girona cycling institution — a long inland loop built around the legendary ascent of Rocacorba. Among cyclists living and training around Girona, Rocacorba is more than just a climb: it is a benchmark, a rite of passage, and one of the purest climbing tests in the region. The route combines fast valley roads, rolling Banyoles terrain, quiet inland Catalunya landscapes, and one long defining summit effort. Local riders often use the challenge of climbing Rocacorba in their age plus ten minutes as a benchmark target.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Tempo Terrain. Leaving Girona, the route begins on wide inland roads, gentle rollers, and fast agricultural valleys. This opening section feels smooth and highly aerobic, making it ideal for group riding and controlled pacing. The terrain allows high average speeds and an efficient warm-up before the major climbing begins. 2. Banyoles Region – Transition Zone. Approaching Banyoles, the route gradually changes character as roads narrow, rollers become steeper, and elevation gain accumulates more noticeably. This area has a distinctly rural Catalunya atmosphere and a quiet endurance-training feel. The roads are highly regarded among Girona cyclists because they flow naturally through the terrain, encourage smooth seated climbing, and rarely become excessively steep. 3. Rocacorba – The Main Event. Rocacorba is one of the most respected climbs in European cycling culture and the defining feature of the route. The climb became legendary because professional riders frequently use it for FTP testing and benchmark efforts. Its gradients are consistent enough for pacing while being long enough to expose weaknesses honestly. Rocacorba rewards patience, cadence control, and disciplined pacing, with early overexertion heavily punished later in the climb. 4. Rocacorba Climb Breakdown. The lower slopes begin sheltered under trees with smooth flowing gradients and a relatively forgiving rhythm, encouraging seated climbing and steady aerobic pacing. The middle section becomes more sustained, mentally demanding, and highly aerobic as fatigue quietly accumulates. Riders settle into prolonged threshold efforts while the road maintains a continuous rhythm. In the final kilometers, the climb changes dramatically with narrower roads, steeper ramps, and increased exposure. These upper sections feel significantly harder than the average gradient suggests and are often psychologically decisive. This is where pacing discipline becomes critical and stronger climbers separate themselves. 5. Descending. The Rocacorba descent is fast and technical in places, particularly in the narrow upper sections where the road surface can be rough and requires concentration and confident bike handling. Lower down, the roads open up, speeds increase significantly, and the descent becomes smoother and more flowing. The return toward Girona is fast, aerodynamic, and heavily influenced by accumulated fatigue. 6. Overall Impression. This is one of the most iconic Girona rides and one of the purest climbing tests in the entire collection. The route feels purpose-built for cyclists, structured entirely around one great climb. It rewards patience, aerobic efficiency, and disciplined climbing while delivering a deeply satisfying experience when ridden well.

routes.title: Sant Hilari Sacalm
routes.description: This route is a classic inland Girona mountain endurance ride centered around the long forest climbs leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Among cyclists riding around Girona, Sant Hilari is famous for endless steady climbing, quiet mountain roads, dense forest scenery, and sustained aerobic riding. This route is smoother, more rhythmic, and less explosive than many Girona climbs, focusing instead on long-duration climbing endurance. It is one of the purest “diesel climber” routes in the collection.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Rolling Terrain. Leaving Girona, the route begins with fast inland roads, gentle rollers, and smooth valley terrain. This opening section feels efficient and highly aerobic, making it ideal for settling into rhythm and group riding dynamics. The roads encourage high average speed and controlled tempo pacing before elevation gradually begins accumulating and climbs become longer and more sustained. 2. Transition Into Mountain Terrain. As the route moves inland, roads narrow, forest density increases, and gradients become steadier. This section has a distinctly remote Catalunya atmosphere with a quiet endurance-riding feel. Unlike coastal terrain, climbs here are largely uninterrupted, rhythm changes are minimal, and efforts become deeply aerobic rather than explosive. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Climb System. The defining feature of the route is the long ascent network toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Typical climbs are sustained and smooth, with gradients mostly ranging between 4–6% and continuous elevation gain over many kilometers. This terrain is famous because roads are exceptionally quiet, climbs are highly consistent, and riders can maintain steady tempo efforts for extended periods. 4. Why Sant Hilari Is So Popular. The Sant Hilari region is widely used for endurance training, tempo climbing blocks, Gran Fondo preparation, and stage-race aerobic conditioning. Unlike steeper Girona climbs, the gradients rarely force explosive efforts. Instead, fatigue builds gradually and deeply, creating a huge aerobic load and a highly professional training-ride atmosphere. 5. Climb Feel. The lower slopes begin gradually through rolling terrain under tree cover, with manageable gradients encouraging smooth seated climbing and steady pacing. In the middle section, climbs become more sustained, recovery sections shorten, and fatigue accumulates steadily. This rewards cadence discipline, controlled breathing, and efficient power delivery. Near the upper elevations, roads become cooler and more exposed, while climbs feel psychologically longer as cumulative fatigue builds. The upper mountain terrain feels calm, remote, and deeply immersive. 6. Environmental Feel. This route delivers one of the strongest forest-road endurance experiences in the collection. Riders can expect dense pine forests, minimal traffic, cool mountain air, and long uninterrupted climbing roads. The atmosphere feels peaceful, rhythmic, and very different from the more dramatic Costa Brava coastal riding. 7. Descents. Descents are long, flowing, and predictable. Compared to coastal descents, they are less technical overall, allow faster average speeds, and provide better recovery opportunities. This supports continuous momentum and efficient endurance pacing on the return toward Girona. 8. Overall Impression. This is one of the smoothest pacing routes and one of the best endurance-climbing experiences in the collection. The challenge is less about attacking steep gradients and more about maintaining rhythm for hours, carefully managing fatigue, and sustaining aerobic output deep into the ride. It is a route that feels significantly bigger than the raw numbers suggest and is deeply satisfying for endurance-focused climbers.

routes.title: St Hilari 125
routes.description: This route is a classic Girona mountain endurance ride built around the long forest ascents leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Compared to other inland routes, this version is more compact than the longest mountain epics while still remaining highly climb-focused, extremely rhythmical, and designed for sustained aerobic output. It is the kind of route that looks manageable on paper but gradually wears riders down through uninterrupted climbing load, delivering a pure diesel-climber experience and a true Girona endurance-training classic.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Rolling Roads. Leaving Girona, the route opens on smooth inland valley roads, gentle rollers, and fast agricultural terrain. This opening section feels efficient and fast-moving, making it ideal for group tempo riding. The early kilometers are comfortable and highly aerobic, feeling significantly easier than the route eventually becomes. 2. Transition Toward Mountain Terrain. As the route heads inland, roads narrow, forest cover increases, and elevation gain becomes more sustained. The riding atmosphere changes dramatically as traffic decreases, climbs become uninterrupted, and the terrain develops a true mountain-road character. This is where the ride quietly begins accumulating fatigue and shifts from tempo riding into sustained climbing mode. 3. Sant Hilari Sacalm Climbing Roads. The route centers around the climb systems leading toward Sant Hilari Sacalm. Typical characteristics include long steady ascents with gradients generally between 4–6% and continuous elevation gain over many kilometers. Unlike steeper Girona climbs, rhythm interruptions are minimal, cadence remains relatively constant, and riders can settle into long aerobic efforts. This terrain is highly valued by professional riders, endurance climbers, and Gran Fondo athletes because it is ideal for aerobic conditioning and sustained pacing. 4. Climbing Feel – Lower Sections. The climbing begins subtly through rolling transitions, gradual uphill drags, and forest-lined roads. The gradients rarely shock the legs but instead slowly increase muscular load over time. This opening climbing phase encourages seated climbing and smooth power delivery rather than explosive efforts. 5. Middle Mountain Terrain. As elevation builds, climbs become more sustained, recovery sections shorten, and fatigue accumulates steadily. This section rewards smooth pacing, efficient seated climbing, and controlled breathing rhythm. The riding style here is defined by pure aerobic endurance rather than repeated high-intensity surges. 6. Upper Slopes. Near the higher elevations, temperatures cool noticeably, roads feel more remote, and climbs become psychologically longer. This is where riders begin feeling cumulative fatigue deeply, making endurance efficiency and pacing discipline increasingly important. 7. Overall Ride Character. St Hilari 125 is one of the strongest examples of Girona’s smooth, rhythm-based mountain riding. The route emphasizes sustained aerobic climbing, quiet forest roads, and gradual fatigue accumulation rather than extreme gradients or explosive efforts. It rewards patience, endurance conditioning, and the ability to maintain consistent tempo over many hours in the saddle.

routes.title: Susqueda Reservoir Olot Loop
routes.description: This route is a deep inland Catalunya mountain endurance loop combining the dramatic roads surrounding Susqueda Reservoir with the volcanic climbing terrain around Olot. Compared to other Girona routes, this ride feels wilder, more remote, more mountainous, and more immersive. It blends long sustained forest climbs, volcanic-region rolling terrain, remote reservoir roads, and continuous aerobic fatigue, making it one of the most adventure-oriented endurance routes in the collection.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Rolling Inland Roads. Leaving Girona, the route begins on smooth inland valley roads, rolling agricultural terrain, and fast aerobic sections. This opening terrain encourages high average speed, feels relatively manageable early on, and allows riders to settle into rhythm before the route gradually becomes more rugged. Over time, climbs lengthen and elevation accumulation accelerates significantly. 2. Susqueda Reservoir Region. The roads surrounding Susqueda Reservoir are among the most dramatic inland roads near Girona. This section features narrow cliffside roads, long reservoir-edge climbs, technical terrain transitions, and remote forest scenery. The riding atmosphere feels wild, isolated, and highly immersive. Unlike the smoother Girona valley roads, rhythm becomes more irregular here, climbs feel psychologically larger, and fatigue accumulates rapidly due to the constant terrain variation. 3. Susqueda Climbs. The reservoir region contains sustained forest climbs, long gradual ascents, and repeated elevation changes. Typical climbing efforts range between 4–8 km with gradients generally around 4–7%, including occasional steeper ramps when exiting reservoir sections. The climbing style is highly aerobic, continuous, and rhythm-based rather than explosively steep. 4. Olot & Volcanic Region Terrain. Approaching Olot, the route transitions into volcanic-region roads, forest-covered mountain terrain, and smoother sustained climbing. This area is famous for its quiet roads, endless rolling mountain terrain, and popularity among professional endurance riders. Climbs in this region flow naturally through the landscape, rarely becoming brutally steep, but steadily creating deep cumulative fatigue over time. 5. Ride Character. The combination of remote reservoir terrain and volcanic mountain roads gives this route a uniquely adventurous feel compared to more traditional Girona loops. Riders experience long periods of uninterrupted aerobic riding, continuous elevation change, and deep endurance fatigue without relying on excessively steep gradients. 6. Descents. Descents vary significantly throughout the route. Around Susqueda Reservoir, roads can be narrow, technical, and highly atmospheric, requiring concentration and careful pacing. In the Olot and Garrotxa regions, descents become smoother, faster, and more flowing, allowing riders to recover while maintaining strong momentum. 7. Overall Impression. This is one of the most immersive and adventurous endurance rides in the collection. The route combines remote Catalunya landscapes, volcanic-region climbing, dramatic reservoir scenery, and sustained aerobic mountain riding into a single highly rewarding experience. It rewards pacing discipline, endurance conditioning, and the ability to manage fatigue over a very long day in the saddle.

routes.title: Tossa de Mar Coastal
routes.description: This route is a classic Costa Brava coastal cycling experience centered around the spectacular Mediterranean roads surrounding Tossa de Mar. Among Girona cyclists, the Tossa coastal roads are legendary for endless ocean views, constant rolling terrain, technical cliffside descents, and repeated short climbing efforts. Compared to inland mountain routes, this ride is more explosive, more rhythmically chaotic, and less about sustained climbing. Instead, it focuses on repeated surges, momentum management, and handling constantly changing terrain. It is one of the purest Girona coastal classics and one of the most scenic endurance rides in the collection.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Inland Roads. Leaving Girona, the route begins on smooth inland valley roads, fast rolling terrain, and gentle aerobic gradients. This opening section encourages high speed, feels efficient and controlled, and often suits group riding well. Gradually, however, the terrain becomes sharper, elevation changes become more frequent, and the influence of the coastline begins to dominate the ride character. 2. Costa Brava Coastal Section. The defining feature of the route is the spectacular terrain surrounding Tossa de Mar. This section includes cliffside roads, technical descents, short repeated climbs, and continuous ocean exposure. Riding here feels dynamic, technical, and highly engaging. Unlike inland Girona routes, there is almost no sustained rhythm. Climbs constantly interrupt momentum and recovery periods remain very short throughout the coastal section. 3. Coastal Rollers. The Costa Brava terrain creates endless short climbs, frequent steep ramps, and constant cadence changes. Typical climbs range between 1–3 km with gradients often reaching 6–10%. Descents are usually short and immediately followed by additional climbing. This creates high normalized power demands, significant muscular fatigue, and constant transitions between aerobic and threshold-level efforts. 4. Sant Grau Region. The route likely includes or approaches terrain associated with Sant Grau d’Ardenya, one of Girona’s most iconic coastal climbs. Sant Grau typically features a 7–8 km ascent with variable gradients and repeated rhythm disruptions throughout the climb. The climb is famous because it perfectly links inland Girona terrain with the Mediterranean coastline while offering exceptional scenery and constantly evolving road character. 5. Descents. Coastal descents throughout the route are technical, twisty, and highly engaging. Riders must manage braking, cornering, and rapid terrain transitions carefully. Unlike inland mountain descents, these roads rarely allow complete recovery because another climb often begins immediately after the descent ends. 6. Ride Character. This route is defined by constant momentum changes, repeated accelerations, and the challenge of maintaining rhythm on highly irregular terrain. It rewards riders who can handle repeated power surges, efficient climbing transitions, and technical descending while managing fatigue over a long endurance ride. 7. Overall Impression. Tossa de Mar Coastal is one of the purest Costa Brava cycling experiences in the collection. Combining dramatic Mediterranean scenery, rolling coastal roads, and relentless terrain changes, it delivers a uniquely engaging endurance ride that feels harder than the raw elevation numbers suggest.

routes.title: Deeeep Deeeep Catalunya
routes.description: This route is a true Catalunya endurance epic — a massive inland adventure combining long rolling valley roads, sustained mountain climbing, remote inland terrain, and endless fatigue accumulation. Compared to shorter Girona loops, this ride feels bigger, wilder, more psychologically demanding, and far more ultra-endurance in character. It is the kind of route that starts comfortably, quietly escalates, and eventually becomes a battle of pacing, fueling, and endurance durability. This is one of the most complete big-day endurance rides and inland Catalunya mountain experiences in the collection. Due to the remote nature of parts of the route, riders should stock up on food and water whenever the opportunity arises.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Early Tempo. Leaving Girona, the route begins on smooth inland roads, gentle rolling terrain, and fast aerobic valley sections. The opening kilometers feel efficient and encourage high speed, which can deceptively hide the scale of the day ahead. This terrain suits tempo riding, group rotation, and controlled aerobic pacing. Gradually, however, climbs lengthen, terrain becomes more remote, and opportunities for passive recovery begin to decrease. 2. Transition Into Deep Inland Catalunya. As the ride progresses, roads narrow, villages become increasingly isolated, and elevation gain becomes more sustained. This is where the route changes character from a standard endurance ride into true mountain-distance terrain. The roads feel quiet, remote, and purpose-built for climbers seeking long uninterrupted efforts. 3. Long Inland Climbing Systems. The route contains multiple sustained climbs, long seated climbing sections, and repeated elevation accumulation over many hours. Typical climbs range from 5–12 km with gradients generally between 4–7%, including occasional steeper upper ramps. Unlike coastal terrain, the climbs here are smoother, the rhythm steadier, and the aerobic load far more relentless over time. 4. Deep Catalunya Rollers. One of the hidden difficulties of the route is the endless rolling terrain between the major climbs. These sections include false flats, long drags, and repeated small elevation gains that feel manageable individually but become deeply exhausting collectively over nearly 150 km. This creates huge cumulative muscular fatigue, continuous aerobic load, and very limited passive recovery opportunities. 5. Descents. Descents throughout the route are long, fast, and flowing. Riders encounter forest-covered corners, high-speed valley returns, and sustained downhill sections between the larger climbs. Compared to coastal descents, these roads are generally less technical, more rhythmical, and better suited for recovery. However, the sheer duration of the ride means fatigue still accumulates heavily despite the smoother terrain. 6. Ride Character – What Makes This Route Hard. This route is difficult not because of one impossible climb or brutally steep gradients, but because of its overall duration, climbing density, endless terrain transitions, and psychological endurance demands. The suffering style is deep, aerobic, progressive, and relentlessly accumulative over time. This is endurance fatigue in its purest form. 7. Overall Impression. This is one of the biggest-feeling rides in the collection and one of the most psychologically demanding endurance routes available. It stands as one of the strongest examples of deep inland Catalunya cycling, delivering a ride experience that feels vast, remote, and quietly relentless. Success on this route depends almost entirely on pacing discipline, fueling strategy, endurance conditioning, and the ability to manage cumulative fatigue over an exceptionally long day in the saddle.

routes.title: Girona → Figueres → Sant Pellaia
routes.description: This route is a classic northern Girona endurance loop combining fast inland valley roads, open Empordà terrain, long rolling endurance sectors, and one of Girona’s most iconic climbs: Sant Pellaia. Compared to the more mountainous routes, this ride is faster, more exposed, more endurance-oriented, and less brutally climb-focused. It has a strong professional base-mile ride feel, with a spring training camp atmosphere and a high-speed aerobic character. It is one of the most versatile and rideable routes in the collection.
routes.detailedDescription: 1. Girona Departure – Fast Inland Tempo. Leaving Girona, the route begins on wide rolling roads, smooth valley terrain, and fast agricultural sectors. This opening phase encourages high average speed, feels efficient and controlled, and is ideal for group riding and rotating pace lines. The terrain rarely becomes steep but constantly rolls, gradually accumulating fatigue over time. 2. Northern Girona & Empordà Terrain. As the route heads north toward Figueres, the landscape becomes more open, more wind-exposed, and generally faster. This region is famous for agricultural plains, long open roads, crosswind exposure, and smooth rolling terrain. The riding style here becomes highly aerodynamic and tempo-oriented, with fatigue driven more by sustained speed and wind resistance than by climbing itself. On windy days, this section can become significantly harder than the elevation numbers suggest. 3. Sant Pellaia. The defining climb of the route is Sant Pellaia, one of the most iconic endurance climbs around Girona. The climb typically measures around 7–8 km with an average gradient of approximately 3–4%, making it smooth, steady, and highly rhythmical. Unlike more explosive Girona climbs, Sant Pellaia is ideal for seated tempo climbing and rarely forces violent efforts. This is why it is heavily used for aerobic endurance work, threshold intervals, and long tempo climbing sessions. 4. Why Sant Pellaia Is So Popular. Sant Pellaia became a Girona classic because its gradient is highly consistent, the road quality is excellent, and traffic levels are generally low. The climb integrates naturally into long endurance loops and rewards pacing discipline, cadence control, and smooth power delivery. Compared to Rocacorba, it is less psychologically brutal but much more suitable for repeated endurance training efforts. 5. Ride Character. This route is defined more by speed, endurance pacing, and sustained aerobic load than by extreme climbing. Riders spend long periods maintaining tempo on exposed roads, managing fatigue through efficient pacing rather than responding to steep gradients. The combination of rolling terrain, open landscapes, and steady climbing creates a highly professional training-ride atmosphere. 6. Descents & Flow. Descents are generally fast, flowing, and predictable, allowing riders to maintain momentum and high average speed throughout the route. Unlike technical coastal descents, these roads favor smooth rhythm and efficient endurance riding rather than aggressive braking and cornering. 7. Overall Impression. Girona → Figueres → Sant Pellaia is one of the most refined endurance rides in the collection. It combines fast rolling roads, open Empordà scenery, sustained aerobic riding, and one of Girona’s best tempo climbs into a route that feels smooth, efficient, and deeply satisfying over long distances.
